title: Super constructor calls with local variables don't work
code_blocks:
class Base;
  function new(int x);
  endfunction
endclass
class Derived extends Base;
  function new;
    int x = 1;
    super.new(x);
  endfunction
endclass
In file included from Vl__ALL.cpp:8:
Vl___024unit__03a__03aDerived__Vclpkg__DepSet_h3f0e46e6__0.cpp: In constructor ‘Vl___024unit__03a__03aDerived::Vl___024unit__03a__03aDerived(Vl__Syms*)’:
Vl___024unit__03a__03aDerived__Vclpkg__DepSet_h3f0e46e6__0.cpp:10:43: error: ‘x’ was not declared in this scope
   10 |     : Vl___024unit__03a__03aBase(vlSymsp, x) {
      |                                           ^
$VERILATOR --binary -O0 file.v

The problem is that `x` variable is referenced in the call of the base class constructor, but the declaration is in the body below.

## Environment:
### Verilator version
(built from this [commit](https://github.com/verilator/verilator/commit/98b8d43a4ae52dba9d42422e61c76c52be5d5c0d))

### **OS**
``Debian 12``
### **g++**

## Solutions
Here we want to propose two potential solutions to this issue - both changing the way objects in ``C++`` are constructed.

Solution will use the following code as an example (it contains more complex cases than the example above).

### Solution 1 - ``init`` method
First solution is to instantiate empty object first and then use ``init()`` method as a proper object setup.

Example ``C++`` code for example above:
It is simple, but I don't like the fact it instantiates empty objects and then fixes them.


### Solution 2 - static factory function
Using static factory function to allow calls before construction of base object and allow sharing variables between before and after super call.
